exercises.title: 671B total, 37B active — why that ratio
exercises.prompt: DeepSeek-V3 is 671B total parameters but only 37B active per token. Explain what total vs active means here, why the ~18:1 ratio is a deliberate bet, and which DeepSeek innovation makes the large total affordable to serve.
exercises.hint: Total = all params stored (MoE experts); active = params used per token (k experts + shared). Big total = capacity/knowledge; small active = cheap compute. MLA shrinks the KV cache so long-context serving stays affordable.
exercises.solution:
**Total vs active:** DeepSeek-V3 is a **Mixture-of-Experts** model (Phase 7.11). **Total parameters (671B)** = everything that must be **stored** — all the experts across all MoE layers, plus attention/embeddings. **Active parameters (37B)** = what actually **computes for a given token** — the shared components plus the **k experts the router selects** for that token (the other experts sit idle for that token). So storage scales with the number of experts, compute scales with k.

**Why the ~18:1 ratio (671/37) is a deliberate bet:** model **knowledge/capacity** tracks roughly with **total** parameters, while **compute cost per token** tracks with **active** parameters. The bet is: **buy huge capacity (671B of knowledge) but pay only 37B of compute per token.** This gives frontier-level capability at the inference FLOP cost of a ~37B dense model — far cheaper than a 671B dense model (which would activate all 671B every token) and far smarter than a 37B dense model. The ratio is tuned so that (a) there are enough experts for rich specialization/capacity, but (b) active params stay small enough for efficient compute and (c) the experts are still each trained enough (not too many to undertrain). It's the MoE thesis — **decouple capacity from compute** — pushed to a frontier configuration.

**What makes the large model affordable to serve:** the catch with big models is the **KV cache** at long context (Lesson 12) — it can dominate memory and bandwidth. DeepSeek-V3's **Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA)** compresses the K/V into a **low-rank latent**, shrinking the KV cache **dramatically** (far below GQA). So even at 128k context, the per-request memory stays manageable, letting you serve the model with reasonable batch sizes and long contexts. (Total **weight** memory is still huge — you need many GPUs to hold 671B — but MLA solves the *per-request long-context* memory that would otherwise make serving impractical, and MoE keeps the *compute* cheap.) So: **MoE makes compute cheap, MLA makes long-context KV cheap** — together they make a 671B model both capable and servable.

exercises.title: MLA: compressing the KV cache
exercises.prompt: Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) is DeepSeek's KV-cache innovation. Explain how it compresses K/V into a latent, why this beats GQA on cache size, and compute the KV footprint at 128k context vs a same-active-param dense model with GQA.
exercises.hint: MLA projects K/V down to a small shared latent vector per token (cached), then up-projects per head at use time. Cache stores the latent (tiny) not full per-head K/V. GQA still caches n_kv_heads × head_dim per token. MLA caches one small latent dim.
exercises.solution:
**How MLA works:** instead of caching full per-head keys and values, MLA **projects the K/V information down into a single small *latent* vector per token** (a low-rank compression shared across heads). That **compressed latent is what gets cached**. At attention time, the latent is **up-projected back into per-head keys and values** on the fly. So the expensive thing you store per token is **one small latent dimension**, not `n_kv_heads × head_dim` worth of K and V. (DeepSeek also decouples a small RoPE component so positions still work.)

**Why it beats GQA:** 
- **GQA** (Phase 7.03) reduces the KV cache by using **fewer KV heads** (e.g. 8 instead of 32) — but it still caches **full key and value vectors for each of those KV heads** per token: cache ∝ `n_kv_heads × head_dim`.
- **MLA** caches only the **compressed latent** (a single low-dimensional vector per token), then reconstructs all heads' K/V from it. The cached size is **the latent dim**, which is **much smaller** than even GQA's reduced `n_kv_heads × head_dim`. So MLA achieves a **larger KV-cache reduction than GQA** while preserving (or improving) quality, because the full per-head K/V is recovered at compute time rather than stored.

**KV footprint at 128k context (illustrative):**
```python
def kv_bytes(per_token_dim, layers, seq, batch=1, bytes=2):
    return 2 * layers * per_token_dim * seq * batch * bytes   # 2 = K and V (or latent)

seq, layers = 128_000, 61
# GQA dense-ish: e.g. 8 KV heads × 128 head_dim = 1024 dims cached per token
gqa = kv_bytes(per_token_dim=8*128, layers=layers, seq=seq)
# MLA: caches a single small latent (e.g. ~512 dim) per token
mla = kv_bytes(per_token_dim=512, layers=layers, seq=seq) / 2  # latent, not K+V sep
print("GQA KV @128k:", gqa/1e9, "GB")
print("MLA KV @128k:", mla/1e9, "GB  (much smaller)")
```
(Exact numbers depend on the published config — DeepSeek reports MLA cutting the KV cache to a small fraction of standard MHA, and notably below GQA, which is what makes **128k context with large batches** affordable.) **The point:** at 128k context the KV cache is the dominant per-request memory term; GQA helps but still caches per-head K/V, while **MLA caches a compressed latent**, so MLA's footprint is **several times smaller than a same-active-param GQA model's** — directly enabling DeepSeek-V3's cheap long-context serving. The cost is a bit of extra compute (the up-projection) and design complexity, but for the memory-bandwidth-bound decode regime (Lesson 12), trading a little compute for a much smaller cache is a clear win.

exercises.title: The four DeepSeek innovations as a co-designed system
exercises.prompt: DeepSeek-V3 turns the six GPT-2 knobs plus four DeepSeek-specific additions: MLA, auxiliary-loss-free load balancing, MTP, and DualPipe. For each, name which part of the architecture/training/inference stack it targets, then explain why MLA + MoE together beat either alone at the frontier, and what DeepSeek-V3 demonstrates about efficient frontier training.
exercises.hint: MLA → inference KV memory. Aux-loss-free routing → training stability of MoE without a balancing loss hurting quality. MTP → training signal + inference drafter. DualPipe → training communication overlap. MLA(cheap KV)+MoE(cheap compute) attack the two different inference bottlenecks. DeepSeek showed frontier models on a modest budget via co-design.
exercises.solution:
**The four DeepSeek-specific innovations and what each targets:**
1. **Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA)** → **inference KV-cache memory/bandwidth.** Compresses K/V into a latent so long-context decode stays cheap (Exercise 2). Attacks the **decode memory bottleneck** (Lesson 12).
2. **Auxiliary-loss-free load balancing** → **MoE training stability/quality.** Standard MoE needs an **auxiliary load-balancing loss** to keep experts evenly used (Phase 7.11), but that loss **competes with the main objective and can hurt quality**. DeepSeek balances experts via **per-expert bias adjustments** (nudging routing) **without an auxiliary loss term**, getting balanced experts *and* full quality. Attacks the **training-objective** stack.
3. **Multi-Token Prediction (MTP)** → **training signal + inference drafter.** A second-token-ahead loss densifies training (better main model) and yields a **high-acceptance speculative drafter** (Lesson 18). Spans the **objective + inference** stack.
4. **DualPipe** → **training communication.** A bidirectional pipeline schedule that **overlaps MoE all-to-all comm with compute**, killing the bubble that the comm-to-compute ratio created (Lesson 19). Attacks the **training-systems** stack.
(Plus the six GPT-2 knobs: RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE, MoE, pre-norm/no-bias, and attention variant — Lesson 14.)

**Why MLA + MoE together beat either alone at the frontier:** they attack the **two different inference bottlenecks** that a frontier model faces, and **neither alone is sufficient**:
- **MoE makes *compute* cheap** (37B active of 671B total) — but MoE does **nothing for the KV cache**; a 671B MoE at 128k context with standard attention would have a **huge KV cache** that dominates memory/bandwidth and makes long-context serving impractical.
- **MLA makes the *KV cache* cheap** — but MLA alone (on a dense model) doesn't give you the **capacity** of 671B params at low compute cost.
Combine them and you get **cheap compute per token (MoE) AND cheap long-context memory (MLA)** simultaneously — frontier capability, low active FLOPs, *and* affordable 128k serving. Either alone leaves one bottleneck unsolved (MoE: KV memory; MLA: capacity-vs-compute). The synergy is why DeepSeek-V3 can be both **very capable and economically servable at long context** — the two innovations cover each other's gaps.

**What DeepSeek-V3 demonstrates about efficient frontier training:** it's the clearest example of **co-designing architecture, objective, training systems, and inference together** to hit frontier quality **on a far smaller budget** than the incumbents:
- The architecture choice (**MoE**) created a **communication problem**, which the training system (**DualPipe**) and routing (**aux-loss-free balancing**) solved.
- The same architecture risked an **inference KV problem**, which **MLA** solved.
- An **objective tweak (MTP)** improved quality *and* handed back an inference speedup.
No single trick is the story — it's that **every layer of the stack was jointly optimized so each one's bottleneck is solved by another**, yielding a 671B-class model trained on ~2,048 H800s at a fraction of typical frontier cost. The lesson: **frontier performance in 2026 isn't bought purely with more GPUs — it's engineered through end-to-end co-design** (architecture ↔ objective ↔ training systems ↔ inference), where understanding *which bottleneck each component targets* lets you assemble a model that's simultaneously capable, trainable, and servable. DeepSeek-V3 is the case study that proves efficient co-design can rival brute-force scale.
